q: The number density of free electrons in copper is nearly <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>28</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. A copper wire has its area of cross-section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and is carrying a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. The drift speed of the electrons is _____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: The number density of free electrons in copper is nearly <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>28</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. A copper wire has its area of cross-section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and is carrying a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. The drift speed of the electrons is _____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
The formula to calculate the current through any conductor is given by

I=nAevd   ...1

Substitute the values of the given parameters into equation (1) and solve to calculate the required drift velocity of the electrons.

3.2=8×1028×2×10-6×1.6×10-19×vd⇒vd=3.216×1.6×1000 m s-1=125×10-6 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: Wheatstone bridge principle is used to measure the specific resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfenced></math> of given wire, having length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math>, radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> is the resistance of wire, then specific resistance is : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>X</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow><mi>L</mi></mfrac></mfenced></math>. If the length of the wire gets doubled then the value of specific resistance will be :
question: Wheatstone bridge principle is used to measure the specific resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfenced></math> of given wire, having length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math>, radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> is the resistance of wire, then specific resistance is : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>X</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow><mi>L</mi></mfrac></mfenced></math>. If the length of the wire gets doubled then the value of specific resistance will be :
solution:
The specific resistance of a material, also known as electrical resistivity, is a measure of how strongly a specific material opposes the flow of electric current.

Specific resistance is an intrinsic property of a material and is dependent on factors such as the material's composition, temperature, and crystal structure. It is the counterpart of conductivity, with which it is inversely proportional.

Hence, even if the length of the wire is doubled, the specific resistance remains the same, i.e., S1.

solution:

q: In a potentiometer experiment, it is found that no current passes through the galvanometer when the terminals of the cell are connected across 52 cm of the potentiometer wire. If the cell is shunted by a resistance of <math><mn>5</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> ,a balance is found when the cell is connected across 40 cm of the wire. question: In a potentiometer experiment, it is found that no current passes through the galvanometer when the terminals of the cell are connected across 52 cm of the potentiometer wire. If the cell is shunted by a resistance of <math><mn>5</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> ,a balance is found when the cell is connected across 40 cm of the wire. Find the internal resistance of the cell.
solution:
Without shunting conditions, 



Es=52×x  .....(i), when, balanced, where, x= potential gradient of wire, with shunting condition



On balancing, Es-Er+Rr=40×x  ....(ii).
On solving:
(i)(ii)⇒11-rr+R=5240
∴r=1.5 Ω

subject: Physics
q: In the given circuit diagram, the currents, I1=-0.3 A, I4=0.8 A and I5=0.4 A, are flowing as shown. The currents I2, I3 and I6, respectively, are:
question: In the given circuit diagram, the currents, I1=-0.3 A, I4=0.8 A and I5=0.4 A, are flowing as shown. The currents I2, I3 and I6, respectively, are:
solution:
Given I1=-0.3A,I4=0.8A,I5=0.4A,in series connection current will be same, hence I5=I6=0.4A,According to kirchoff's juncion rule, algebraic sum of the currents meeting at tthe juction is zero.i.e currents flowing into the junction is taken as positive and current flowing out of the junction is taken as negative.At the junction R, -I4+I2+I1=0⇒-0.8+I2-0.3=0⇒I2=1.1AAt junction Q, I6+I3-I2-I1=0⇒0.4+I3-1.1+0.3=0⇒I3=1.1-0.7=0.4AIt is problem of kirchhof's current law.

I6=0.4 A

I3=0.8 A-O.4A=0.4 A

I2=0.8+0.3A=1.1 A

The voltage of electric main supply is <math><mn>220</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> . The minimum fuse capacity (rated value) of the building will be:
question: In a building there are <math><mn>15</mn></math> bulbs of <math><mn>45</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> , <math><mn>15</mn></math> bulbs of <math><mn>100</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mn>15</mn></math> small fans of <math><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> and <math><mn>2</mn></math> heaters of <math><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> . The voltage of electric main supply is <math><mn>220</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> . The minimum fuse capacity (rated value) of the building will be:
solution:
Total power is 15×45+15×100+15×10+2×1000
=4325W
So current =4325220=19.66 A≈ 20 A

so we have to use a fuse which can tolerate atleast 20 A current.

q: Equivalent resistance between the adjacent corners of a regular <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math>-sided polygon of uniform wire of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> would be :
question: Equivalent resistance between the adjacent corners of a regular <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math>-sided polygon of uniform wire of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> would be :
solution:
Given the total resistance of the wire is R. If the polygon has n numbers of side, then the resistance r, as shown in the following figure, of each side can be written as

r= Rn.................(1)

Among all the resistances, n-1 resistances are connected in series combination, whereas, their equivalent resistance is connected in parallel with the nth resistance. The equivalent circuit diagram for the above set of resistances can be depicted as follows:

From the above equivalent circuit diagram, the net equivalent resistance Req for the entire combination can be calculated as follows-

1Req= 1n-1r+1r= 1n-1Rn+1Rn= nn-1R+nR= n2n-1RReq= n-1Rn2

q: Current measured by the ammeter A in the reported circuit when no current flows through 10Ω resistance, will be _____ A.
question: Current measured by the ammeter A in the reported circuit when no current flows through 10Ω resistance, will be _____ A.
solution:
This is a Wheatstone circuit. For the current to be zero in 10 Ω the Wheatstone circuit should be balanced. Therefore,

R3=46⇒R=2 Ω

Now, the equivalent resistance across the battery will be, 

Req=2+4×3+62+4+3+6=3.6 Ω

Therefore, the current measured by the ammeter is i=VR=363.6=10 A.

subject: Physics
q: Refer to the circuit diagram given below. The heat generated across the $6\,\Omega$ resistance in $100$ second is $\dfrac{\alpha}{100}$ J. The value of $\alpha$ is _______. (Nearest integer)
question: Refer to the circuit diagram given below. The heat generated across the $6\,\Omega$ resistance in $100$ second is $\dfrac{\alpha}{100}$ J. The value of $\alpha$ is _______. (Nearest integer)
solution:
Taking the right-hand wire as the reference node at $0\text{ V}$, let the left junction (where the $3\,\Omega$, $4\,\Omega$ and $6\,\Omega$ resistors meet) be Node A at potential $V_A$.

Bottom branch: The positive terminal of the $2\text{ V}$ battery is at the reference, so its negative terminal (and hence the left end of the $3\,\Omega$ resistor) is at $-2\text{ V}$.
Top branch: The positive terminal of the $3\text{ V}$ battery is at the reference, so its negative terminal (and hence the node between the $6\,\Omega$ resistor and the $3\text{ V}$ battery) is at $-3\text{ V}$.

Applying KCL at Node A (sum of currents leaving the node is zero):
$\dfrac{V_A - 0}{4} + \dfrac{V_A - (-2)}{3} + \dfrac{V_A - (-3)}{6} = 0$

$\dfrac{V_A}{4} + \dfrac{V_A + 2}{3} + \dfrac{V_A + 3}{6} = 0$

Multiplying throughout by $12$:
$3V_A + 4(V_A + 2) + 2(V_A + 3) = 0$

$3V_A + 4V_A + 8 + 2V_A + 6 = 0$

$9V_A + 14 = 0 \Rightarrow V_A = -\dfrac{14}{9}\text{ V}$

Current through the $6\,\Omega$ resistor (from Node A towards the $-3\text{ V}$ point):
$I_6 = \dfrac{V_A - (-3)}{6} = \dfrac{-\dfrac{14}{9} + 3}{6} = \dfrac{\dfrac{13}{9}}{6} = \dfrac{13}{54}\text{ A}$

Heat generated in the $6\,\Omega$ resistor in $t = 100\text{ s}$:
$H = I_6^2 \cdot R \cdot t = \left(\dfrac{13}{54}\right)^2 \cdot 6 \cdot 100$

$H = \dfrac{169}{2916} \cdot 600 = \dfrac{169 \cdot 100}{486} = \dfrac{16900}{486}\text{ J}$

Given $H = \dfrac{\alpha}{100}\text{ J}$:
$\dfrac{\alpha}{100} = \dfrac{16900}{486}$

$\alpha = \dfrac{16900 \cdot 100}{486} = \dfrac{1690000}{486} \approx 3477.37$

Rounding to the nearest integer:
$\alpha = 3477$

Hence, the answer is $3477$.

subject: Physics
q: A galvanmeter has a coil of resistance $200 \Omega$ with a full scale deflection at $20 \mu \mathrm{A}$. The value of resistance to be added to use it as an ammeter of range $(0-20) \mathrm{mA}$ is ;
question: A galvanmeter has a coil of resistance $200 \Omega$ with a full scale deflection at $20 \mu \mathrm{A}$. The value of resistance to be added to use it as an ammeter of range $(0-20) \mathrm{mA}$ is ;
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{G}=200 \Omega \\ & \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{g}}=20 \mu \mathrm{A} \\ & \mathrm{i}=\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{g}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{G}}{\mathrm{S}}+1\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 20 \times 10^{-3}=20 \times 10^{-6}\left(\frac{200}{\mathrm{~S}}+1\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{200}{\mathrm{~S}}=999 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{S} \approx 0.2 \Omega\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: To determine the resistance $(R)$ of a wire, a circuit is designed below. The $V-I$ characteristic curve for this circuit is plotted for the voltmeter and the ammeter readings as shown in figure. The value of $R$ is ______ $\Omega$.
question: To determine the resistance $(R)$ of a wire, a circuit is designed below. The $V-I$ characteristic curve for this circuit is plotted for the voltmeter and the ammeter readings as shown in figure. The value of $R$ is ______ $\Omega$.
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Req }=\frac{10^4 R}{10^4+R} \\ & E=4 V, I=2 m A \\ & I=\frac{E}{R e q} \Rightarrow 2 \times 10^{-3}=\frac{4\left(10^4+R\right)}{10^4 R} \\ & \Rightarrow 20 R=40000+4 R\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & 16 R=40000 \\ & R=2500 \Omega\end{aligned}$

question: For the circuit shown, with R1=1.0 Ω , R2=2.0 Ω , E1=2 V and E2=E3=4 V, the potential difference between the points ‘a’ and ‘b’ is approximately ( in V ):
solution:
If the electric potential at point a is Va and the electric potential at point b is Vb then
Vb-Va=22+42+4212+12+12
Vb-Va=1+2+232=103

∴Vb-Va=3.3 V

question: The total current supplied to the circuit as shown in figure by the 5 V battery is _____ A.
solution:
As in this case, 5 Ω & 5 Ω are in parallel hence, their combined resistance value will be 2.5 Ω. Now, this 2.5 Ω with existing 2.5 Ω are in series and hence can be combined to write 5 Ω.

In the final diagram, we can write equivalent resistance across the battery as 2.5 Ω.

Hence, current through the battery is, i=5 V2.5 Ω=2 A

q: Resistance are connected in a meter bridge circuit as shown in the figure. The balancing length l1 is 40 cm. Now an unknown resistance x is connected in series with P and new balancing length is found to be 80 cm measured from the same end. Then the value of x will be _____ Ω.
question: Resistance are connected in a meter bridge circuit as shown in the figure. The balancing length l1 is 40 cm. Now an unknown resistance x is connected in series with P and new balancing length is found to be 80 cm measured from the same end. Then the value of x will be _____ Ω.
solution:
Applying balanced condition for meter bridge

Initially, PQ=40 cm60 cm=23       ...1

Finally, P+xQ=80 cm20 cm=41      ...2

Dividing equation 2 by (1,

P+xP=4×32=6

⇒1+xP=6⇒xP=5

∴  x=5P=5×4=20 Ω

subject: Physics
q: A heater is designed to operate with a power of $1000 \mathrm{~W}$ in a $100 \mathrm{~V}$ line. It is connected in combination with a resistance of $10 \Omega$ and a resistance $R$, to a $100 \mathrm{~V}$ mains as shown in figure. For the heater to operate at $62.5 \mathrm{~W}$, the value of $\mathrm{R}$ should be _____$\Omega$.
question: A heater is designed to operate with a power of $1000 \mathrm{~W}$ in a $100 \mathrm{~V}$ line. It is connected in combination with a resistance of $10 \Omega$ and a resistance $R$, to a $100 \mathrm{~V}$ mains as shown in figure. For the heater to operate at $62.5 \mathrm{~W}$, the value of $\mathrm{R}$ should be _____$\Omega$.
solution:
$\mathrm{R}_{\text {heater }}=\frac{\mathrm{V}^2}{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{(100)^2}{1000}=10 \Omega$ For heater $P=\frac{V^2}{R} \Rightarrow V=\sqrt{P R}$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{V}=\sqrt{62.5 \times 10} \\ & \mathrm{~V}=25 \mathrm{v}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & i_1=\frac{75}{10}=7.5 \mathrm{~A}, \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{H}}=\frac{25}{10}=2.5 \mathrm{~A} . \\ & \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{R}}=\mathrm{i}_1-\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{H}}=5 \\ & \mathrm{~V}=\mathrm{IR} \\ & \mathrm{R}=\frac{25}{5}=5 \Omega\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: An electric bulb rated $50 \mathrm{~W}-200 \mathrm{~V}$ is connected across a $100 \mathrm{~V}$ supply. The power dissipation of the bulb is:
question: An electric bulb rated $50 \mathrm{~W}-200 \mathrm{~V}$ is connected across a $100 \mathrm{~V}$ supply. The power dissipation of the bulb is:
solution:
Rated power \& voltage gives resistance
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{V}^2}{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{(200)^2}{50}=\frac{40000}{50} \\
& \mathrm{R}=800 \\
& \mathrm{P}=\frac{\left(\mathrm{V}_{\text {applied }}\right)^2}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{(100)^2}{800} \\
& \mathrm{P}=12.5 \text { watt }
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: An electrical bulb rated <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>, is connected in series with another bulb rated <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>. If the voltage across combination is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, the power consumed by the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> bulb will be about _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>.
question: An electrical bulb rated <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>, is connected in series with another bulb rated <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>. If the voltage across combination is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, the power consumed by the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> bulb will be about _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>.
solution:
Electric power is given by P=V2R, here, V is voltage and R is resistance.

Now, for each bulb, R1=V2P=2202100=484 Ω and  R2=V2P=220260=484106 Ω

Net current through the series combination is I=220484+484×106 A=0.171 A

Hence, the power consumed by the 100 W bulb is P1=I2R1=14.06 W

q: In the circuit shown, the resistance r is a variable resistance. If for r=fR , the heat generation in r is maximum then the value of f is
question: In the circuit shown, the resistance r is a variable resistance. If for r=fR , the heat generation in r is maximum then the value of f is
solution:
Power in “r”

P= I 2 2 r= [ I× R R+r ] 2 r= I 2 R 2  r ( R+r ) 2

P= [ E R+ R×fR R+fR ] 2 R 2 ( fR ) ( R+fR ) 2 = E 2 R 2 fR R 2 [ 1+ f ( f+1 ) ] 2 R 2 ( 1+f ) 2

P= E 2 f R ( 1+2f ) 2

For P( max )  dP df =0

E 2 R =[ ( 1+2f ) 2 −f{ 2( 1+2f )( 0+2 ) } ( 1+2f ) 2 ]=0

( 1+2f ) 2 =4f( 1+2f )

1+2f=4f

f= 1 2

subject: Physics
q: In a Wheatstone's bridge, there resistances $\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{Q}$ and $\mathrm{R}$ connected in the three arms and the fourth arm is formed by two resistances $\mathrm{S}_1$ and $\mathrm{S}_2$ connected in parallel. The condition for bridge to be balanced will be
question: In a Wheatstone's bridge, there resistances $\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{Q}$ and $\mathrm{R}$ connected in the three arms and the fourth arm is formed by two resistances $\mathrm{S}_1$ and $\mathrm{S}_2$ connected in parallel. The condition for bridge to be balanced will be
solution: $\frac{\mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{Q}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}\left(\mathrm{S}_1+\mathrm{S}_2\right)}{\mathrm{S}_1 \mathrm{~S}_2}$

subject: Physics
q: An elliptical loop having resistance R, of semi major axis a , and semi minor axis b  is placed in a magnetic field as shown in the figure. If the loop is rotated about the x-axis with angular frequency ω, the average power loss in the loop due to Joule heating is :
question: An elliptical loop having resistance R, of semi major axis a , and semi minor axis b  is placed in a magnetic field as shown in the figure. If the loop is rotated about the x-axis with angular frequency ω, the average power loss in the loop due to Joule heating is :
solution:
The expression for induced EMF in the rotating loop in uniform magnetic field is given by

ε=NABω cosωt

The average power loss

P=<ε2R>=<A2B2ω2cos2ωtR>

<cos2θ>=12

<P>=A2B2ω2R12

⇒ <P>=π2a2b2B22Rω2

q: A 10 V battery with internal resistance 1 Ω and a 15 V battery with internal resistance 0.6 Ω are connected in parallel to a voltmeter (see figure). The reading in the voltmeter will be close to:
question: A 10 V battery with internal resistance 1 Ω and a 15 V battery with internal resistance 0.6 Ω are connected in parallel to a voltmeter (see figure). The reading in the voltmeter will be close to:
solution:
The equivalent emf of the battery parallel combination is given as

Equation = E1r1  + E2r21r1 + 1r2

= 101 + 150.61 1 + 1 0.6= 10+ 15061+ 106 

=1058

=13.1 V

∴    The reading measured by voltmeter =13.1 V

q: The length of a potentiometer wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and it carries a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> For a cell of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mo> </mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and internal resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> the null point on it is found to be at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> The resistance of whole wire is:
question: The length of a potentiometer wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and it carries a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> For a cell of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mo> </mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and internal resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> the null point on it is found to be at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> The resistance of whole wire is:
solution:
Potential gradient =51000=VP1200
VP=6 V
And RP=VPI=660×10-3=100 Ω

q: An ideal battery of emf <math><mn>4</mn><mi>V</mi></math> and resistance <math><mi>R</mi></math> are connected in series in the primary circuit of a potentiometer of length <math><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> and resistance <math><mn>5</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> to give a potential difference of <math><mn>5</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mi>V</mi></math> across <math><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> of potentiometer wire, is:
question: An ideal battery of emf <math><mn>4</mn><mi>V</mi></math> and resistance <math><mi>R</mi></math> are connected in series in the primary circuit of a potentiometer of length <math><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> and resistance <math><mn>5</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> to give a potential difference of <math><mn>5</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mi>V</mi></math> across <math><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> of potentiometer wire, is:
solution:
Potential gradient x=5×10-310×10-2=4R+5×51

⇒  120=20R+5

⇒  400=R+5

R=395 Ω

subject: Physics
q: The voltage and the current between $A$ and $B$ points shown in the circuit are _____.
question: The voltage and the current between $A$ and $B$ points shown in the circuit are _____.
solution:
The four parallel branches between the common left node $A$ and the common right node are all identical: each consists of a $27\text{ V}$ source in series with a $3\ \Omega$ resistor. In the third branch, the two cells $(14\text{ V}$ and $13\text{ V})$ aid each other, giving a net EMF of $14 + 13 = 27\text{ V}$.

Thevenin equivalent between $A$ and $B$:
Since all four branches have the same EMF and polarity in parallel:
$V_{th} = 27\text{ V}$

The four $3\ \Omega$ resistors in parallel give:
$R_{th} = \dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75\ \Omega$

Load branch (diagonal):
The diagonal connecting $A$ and $B$ consists of two $3\ \Omega$ resistors in series, so:
$R_L = 3 + 3 = 6\ \Omega$

Current through the diagonal branch:
$I = \dfrac{V_{th}}{R_{th} + R_L} = \dfrac{27}{0.75 + 6} = \dfrac{27}{6.75} = 4\text{ A}$

Voltage across $A$ and $B$:
$V_{AB} = I \cdot R_L = 4 \times 6 = 24\text{ V}$

Hence, the voltage is $24\text{ V}$ and the current is $4\text{ A}$, which corresponds to option $(2)\ 24\text{ V},\ 4\text{ A}$.

subject: Physics
q: A wire of uniform resistance $\lambda \Omega / \mathrm{m}$ is bent into a circle of radius $r$ and another piece of wire with length $2 r$ is connected between points $A$ and $B(A O B)$ as shown in figure. The equivalent resistance between points $A$ and $B$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
question: A wire of uniform resistance $\lambda \Omega / \mathrm{m}$ is bent into a circle of radius $r$ and another piece of wire with length $2 r$ is connected between points $A$ and $B(A O B)$ as shown in figure. The equivalent resistance between points $A$ and $B$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
solution:
The wire bent into a circle of radius $r$ has resistance $\lambda \cdot \pi r$ for the semicircular arcs, and the straight wire of length $2r$ has resistance $\lambda \cdot 2r$. The three paths between A and B (two semicircular arcs of the circle and one straight wire through center) are in parallel.

$\frac{1}{R_{AB}} = \frac{2}{\lambda \pi r} + \frac{1}{\lambda \cdot 2r} + \frac{2}{\lambda \cdot 3\pi r}$

$= \frac{1}{\lambda r} \left[\frac{2}{\pi} + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{2}{3\pi}\right]$

$= \frac{1}{\lambda r} \left(\frac{12 + 3\pi + 4}{6\pi}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda r} \cdot \frac{16 + 3\pi}{6\pi}$

$R_{AB} = \lambda r \left(\frac{6\pi}{16 + 3\pi}\right) = \frac{6\pi \lambda r}{3\pi + 16}$

subject: Physics
q: In the given circuit, the current in resistance R3 is:
question: In the given circuit, the current in resistance R3 is:
solution:
Equivalent resistance, Req=2 Ω+4×44+4 Ω+1 Ω=5 Ω

Current through battery, i=VReq=105=2 A

Current in resistance R3=2×44+4

=2×48

=1 A

question: A square-shaped wire with a resistance of each side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is bent to form a complete circle. The resistance between two diametrically opposite points of the circle in a unit of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> will is,  _____.
solution:
We know that in a parallel combination of resistors the net resistance, 1RP=1R1+1R2+1R3+.... and when they are connected in series then net resistance is, R=R1+R2+R3+......+Rn.  A square-shaped wire with a resistance of each side is, 3 Ω, then the circuit is, 
then the net resistance is 3 Ω.

subject: Physics
q: A moving coil of galvanometer when shunted with $2\,\Omega$ resistance gives a full scale deflection for a current of $500$ mA. When a resistance of $470\,\Omega$ is connected in series it gives a full scale deflection for $10$ V potential applied on it. The value of resistance of galvanometer coil is __________ $\Omega$.
question: A moving coil of galvanometer when shunted with $2\,\Omega$ resistance gives a full scale deflection for a current of $500$ mA. When a resistance of $470\,\Omega$ is connected in series it gives a full scale deflection for $10$ V potential applied on it. The value of resistance of galvanometer coil is __________ $\Omega$.
solution:
Let $G$ be the resistance of the galvanometer and $I_g$ be the current required for full scale deflection.

When the galvanometer is shunted with $S = 2\,\Omega$, it acts as an ammeter of range $I = 500\text{ mA} = 0.5\text{ A}$. The potential difference across the galvanometer and the shunt is equal:

$I_g G = (I - I_g) S$

$I_g G = (0.5 - I_g) \times 2$

$I_g G + 2 I_g = 1$

$I_g = \dfrac{1}{G + 2}$

When a resistance $R = 470\,\Omega$ is connected in series, it acts as a voltmeter of range $V = 10\text{ V}$. The total voltage is the sum of the voltage drops:

$V = I_g (G + R)$

$10 = I_g (G + 470)$

$I_g = \dfrac{10}{G + 470}$

Equating the two expressions for $I_g$:

$\dfrac{1}{G + 2} = \dfrac{10}{G + 470}$

$G + 470 = 10(G + 2)$

$G + 470 = 10G + 20$

$9G = 450$

$G = 50\,\Omega$

Answer: $50$
